items.title: Novel antibiotic candidates starve resistant bacteria by blocking vitamin supply
items.summary: Bacteria have spent decades evolving resistance to virtually every antibiotic we have thrown at them. To stay ahead, new drugs must hit targets that existing antibiotics have never touched. A research team at the Helmholtz Institute for Pharmaceutical Research Saarland (HIPS) has now developed a ser

items.title: Researcher shows how AI can personalize learning for homeschooling families
items.summary: More than 3.4 million American children, or about 6% of the school-age population, now learn at home, and that group has grown at nearly three times the pre-pandemic rate. Homeschooling is also increasingly popular in countries including Canada and the United Kingdom, as more parents look to customi

items.title: Atomic channels can separate rare earth elements from each other without using toxic chemicals
items.summary: Rare earth elements like lanthanum, neodymium and dysprosium are used to build the electric motor in your car, the LED lights in your house and the MRI machine at your doctor's office. But first, they have to be mined and separated from each other. Historically, that purification has been a dif

items.title: Researchers develop automated system to accelerate outbreak detection and disease surveillance
items.summary: Biomedical engineers at Brown University have developed a fully automated workflow that simplifies and accelerates the preparation of bacterial samples for genetic sequencing. The method, dubbed Pathogen2Read, streamlines a critical bottleneck in the genetic sequencing process and could enable small

items.title: Animals in prehistoric paintings reveal hidden pattern across nine Basque caves
items.summary: Iñaki Intxaurbe, a researcher at the EHU-University of the Basque Country, analyzed the distribution of images in nine caves in the Basque Country using network-based analysis and statistical techniques.

items.title: Family courts must do more to hear children's voices, study says
items.summary: Children involved in private family law proceedings in England can experience serious harm to their mental and physical health because their voices are too often ignored or dismissed, according to new research led by the University of Manchester in partnership with SHERA Research Group members and T

items.title: Human eyes needed, not AI, to study the Arctic sky
items.summary: Scientists studying space weather are turning to human volunteers rather than artificial intelligence to help sort through hundreds of thousands of images of the Arctic sky. The Clear Skies—Kiruna project is asking members of the public to look at pictures of the sky above Kiruna, Sweden, and say wh

items.title: Sleeping sickness parasite reveals enzyme weak spot that could guide safer drugs
items.summary: African sleeping sickness and related diseases can be treated only with toxic and sometimes ineffective drugs. Now, University of Connecticut researchers show in Nucleic Acids Research that a crucial enzyme could be the key to safer, more effective medicines.

items.title: As Supreme Court decisions grow sharper, new study examines justices' calculated concessions
items.summary: Recent U.S. Supreme Court terms have seen justices openly accuse one another of rewriting statutes, ignoring precedent and substituting policy preferences for law. Whether in disputes over presidential immunity, administrative power, nationwide injunctions or transgender rights, justices have demons

items.title: Understudied dogs may reshape what science knows about canine behavior
items.summary: Dogs are humans' best friends in many places across the globe. But does a dog in New York City respond in the same way as a dog in Kyoto or Buenos Aires?

items.title: Extreme precipitation in Central Asia connected to drought in mainland Southeast Asia
items.summary: In much of Asia, the effects of climate change are felt through an increased frequency of extreme weather events, especially water-related ones. Droughts are becoming more common in mainland Southeast Asia, while periods with unusually large amounts of rainfall are increasing in Central Asia. Rice U

items.title: Fragmented system hampering UK response to economic crime, researchers find
items.summary: A new study from The University of Manchester has offered the most comprehensive look to date at how the U.K. coordinates its response to economic crime, identifying the structural challenges that make this difficult to do well. The study examines the tensions that make these challenges resistant to

items.title: Vapor-controlled supramolecules can switch color, glow and phase
items.summary: Controlling interactions between molecules and their functions from the molecular level to the macroscopic scale is challenging in supramolecular and materials chemistry. To this end, a research team led by associate professor Yosuke Tani from WPI-ITbM at Nagoya University and Keisuke Wada from Kyot

items.title: Study finds river sand mining is outstripping nature's supply
items.summary: The sand used to build cities, roads and other infrastructure is being removed from many rivers faster than nature can replace it, according to a global review led by researchers from Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore).

items.title: New radio-burst method helps locate universe's missing ordinary matter
items.summary: Stars and galaxies make up much of the universe's ordinary, observable matter. But for decades, scientists have wrestled with a cosmic conflict: There should be much more.

items.title: Researchers call for a shift in crop development to tackle hunger, malnutrition, and climate change
items.summary: Feeding a growing global population is no longer enough. Scientists say the next generation of crops must also deliver better nutrition while withstanding the increasing impacts of climate change.

items.title: Hawaiian stilt reclassified from endangered to threatened after 50 years of conservation efforts
items.summary: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service announced the reclassification of the Aʻeo, or Hawaiian stilt (Himantopus mexicanus knudseni), from endangered to threatened under the Endangered Species Act, a rare and hopeful milestone for a native Hawaiian species. The change, published in the Federal Register

items.title: US-India Satellite Delivers Data, Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica
items.summary: As of July 20, the public can access data from the two powerful radar instruments aboard the NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite. Teams in the United States and India will release files processed from the satellite’s L-band and S-band radars on an ongoing basis, helping researchers

items.title: New insights into how DNA, the body's instruction manual for life, is regulated
items.summary: Scientists at the Peter MacCallum Cancer Center have uncovered new insights into how cells regulate DNA methylation, a fundamental process that helps keep potentially harmful viral elements in our genome under control.

items.title: NASA Langley Celebrates Community through Music with ‘Symphony Under the Stars’ Event
items.summary: NASA Langley’s “Stars, Stripes, and Supernovas – Symphony Under the Stars” event brought employees, families, and community members together for an evening filled with music, connection, and celebration.   Organizers planned the event, which took place July 16, to mark the 250th&

items.title: A new compact instrument enables high-fidelity measurements of energetic particles on CubeSats
items.summary: A team of NASA-sponsored scientists and engineers has developed a novel approach to observing high-energy particles in the near-Earth space environment, incorporating miniaturized sensors into a compact, multiview particle-detection instrument unlike any before it.

items.title: Frozen fiber couples light and sound 1,000 times more strongly than standard glass fibers
items.summary: Researchers have developed a new type of optical fiber by freezing a glass capillary filled with liquid. It guides light and sound waves simultaneously and enables highly efficient coupling between them. The high coupling strength lowers the energy consumption of photonic neuromorphic computing sche

items.title: Study shows nutrient loss in vineyards can be attributed to factors beyond fruit harvest
items.summary: New research from Washington State University could help grape growers improve nutrient use efficiency and develop a more accurate fertilizer budget, helping ensure high fruit quality, vineyard productivity and long-term economic and environmental sustainability.

items.title: New exhibition reveals 10,000 years of prehistoric life in the Trent Valley
items.summary: A new exhibition at the University of Nottingham Museum is bringing the prehistoric story of the Trent Valley to life, showcasing archaeological discoveries that are transforming understanding of one of Britain's most significant river landscapes.

items.title: Zoo elephant survival has improved substantially over 6 decades, international study finds
items.summary: Elephants living in modern zoos are living significantly longer than they did half a century ago, according to a study published in the journal Scientific Reports. The research—led by ZSL (the Zoological Society of London) in collaboration with Species360, the University of Southern Denmark and the

items.title: Urgent action is needed to protect Somalia from a potentially unprecedented weather event, experts warn
items.summary: Cardiff University leads a multidisciplinary research collaboration representing academia and multiple nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) to explore Somalia's 2023 El Niño floods and lessons learned from a similar event in 1997. The research, which has yet to undergo peer review, was released

items.title: Antarctic 12-m Terahertz Telescope project to uncover hidden galaxy formation
items.summary: A new simulation study suggests that the Antarctic 12-m Terahertz Telescope (ATT12), a next-generation facility led by University of Tsukuba, could uncover between 1 and 10 million dusty star-forming galaxies throughout cosmic history.

items.title: Znanstvenici: Naša galaksija možda se okrenula na bok
items.summary: NOVE simulacije pokazuju da se disk Mliječne staze možda nagnuo na bok nakon sudara s drugom galaksijom. Ovaj događaj objašnjava zašto zvijezde u halou naše galaksije rotiraju sporije od očekivanog.

items.title: Low cloud-cover has helped mute warming for the last 47 years, but models predict this will change
items.summary: A recent study, published in Geophysical Research Letters, combines historical satellite observations with atmospheric reanalysis data to construct a multidecade record of low-cloud cover in the hope of improving current climate models.

items.title: Record fires killed nearly 1 in 5 mature giant sequoias, but controlled burns can boost survival
items.summary: A new study found that giant sequoias were nearly four times as likely to survive extreme wildfires in forests managed with prescribed burns, which decrease tree mortality. The work is published in the journal Nature Communications.

items.title: Is there really a global loneliness epidemic? Research reveals a more complex picture
items.summary: Few human experiences are as universal—or as poorly understood—as loneliness. More than just feeling blue and the inspiration for countless songs, it's linked to poorer well-being, worse mental and physical health and a higher risk of early death.

items.title: Reinventing food packaging with safe and sustainable oxygen-scavenging biodegradable plastic
items.summary: From ensuring that food remains fresh longer to extending its shelf life for future consumption, food packaging plays an indispensable role in modern society. Conventional food packaging plastics are sturdy and highly effective at keeping out oxygen, one of the main drivers of food spoilage.

items.title: When a SpaceX rocket crashes into the moon, scientists will be watching
items.summary: On Aug. 5, 2026, an abandoned piece of space junk is predicted to crash into the moon near a spot called Einstein Crater. Astronomers will be watching because the collision and aftermath may be visible from Earth.

items.title: How sea anemone cells reform into an organism
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Vienna have discovered a key mechanism that enables sea anemones to regenerate into fully developed organisms from disorganized clusters of cells. The study, published in Nature Communications, shows that the so-called Notch signaling pathway controls tissue organiza

items.title: Google wants to release 16 million sterile mosquitoes: Australian tests offer a valuable lesson
items.summary: In the United States, Google's, now Alphabet's, Debug initiative has asked the federal government for permission to release up to 32 million sterilized male mosquitoes in California and Florida.

items.title: Over 10,000 traps reveal only 1 pookila in southeast Queensland survey
items.summary: It's 5 a.m. when we crawl out of our tents. The catbirds are calling and the night sky is fading to dawn as we prepare to head into the wilderness. We're surrounded by the towering eucalypts of southeast Queensland in search of a small, native mouse called the pookila.

items.title: Jurassic sea monster was smaller than once thought, but no less formidable
items.summary: For more than 25 years, Liopleurodon has been one of the world's most recognizable prehistoric marine predators. Immortalized in the BBC's landmark Walking with Dinosaurs series as a colossal 25-meter (82-foot) marine hunter, it captured the imagination of millions and became synonymous wi

items.title: Finding Venus zone planets with PLATO will show us how habitable planets evolve
items.summary: Dreams of finding a sort of Earth 2.0 motivate much of the thinking around the search for exoplanets. But there is more to exoplanet science than searching for potentially habitable worlds. Scientists need a deeper understanding of the overall exoplanet population to understand the cosmos.

items.title: Burnt wooden beams discovered in Jerusalem shed new light on the First Temple's destruction
items.summary: A remarkable discovery has been made at the Givati Parking Lot excavations in the City of David, within Jerusalem Walls National Park. Excavations led by the Israel Antiquities Authority and Tel Aviv University uncovered massive wooden beams that collapsed and burned during the destruction of the Fi

items.title: AI awareness in Nebraska differs by age, education, income
items.summary: Artificial intelligence is being adopted rapidly across industries, workplaces and personal use, with an estimated 1 in 4 Americans using it regularly, but there is a knowledge gap.

items.title: Scientists use dual-frequency images to explore black hole plasma physics
items.summary: The first-ever image of a black hole—a glowing, ring-like structure at the heart of galaxy M87—was unveiled to the world in a landmark moment in 2019. Now, researchers have taken the next big step—moving beyond capturing a picture of the black hole to understanding its physics through dual-frequency

items.summary: Paul StankusBrookhaven Science Associates  The scientific goal is to enable a new method for observing gravitational waves at low frequencies, based on the astrometric GW signature — gravitational waves passing by the Earth will cause a (very small) coordinated apparent motion of all sky

items.summary: Paul StankusBrookhaven Science Associates The scientific goal of the proposed work will be reconstructing the image, ie resolving surface features, of an Earth-like exoplanet around a nearby star as seen in visible light. The innovation is in two stages. First, the design of a new kind of nulli

items.summary: Pablo Sobron SanchezSETI Institute This proposal explores a new class of reconnaissance spacecraft that map minerals from orbit using Raman spectroscopy during high-speed flybys–without landing, sample return, or extended dwell. If feasible, this concept would enable NASA to evaluate ice and i

items.summary: David SmithDuke University  The increasing population of spacefaring vehicles and satellites motivates increasingly powerful technologies for space situational awareness (SSA). While the US space surveillance network (SSN) is able to monitor objects down to about four inches in size using,

items.title: Photophoretic Tracers for Near-Space Remote Sensing at 30-100 km Altitudes
items.summary: Benjamin SchaferRarified Technologies, Inc. We propose a new approach to atmospheric sensing at 30-100 km altitudes using photophoretically levitating tracers. These lightweight structures harness sunlight to remain suspended for up to months at controlled altitudes and can be remotely tracked

items.title: Actively Steerable Femtosat Constellations for In-situ Exploration of Saturn’s Rings, Atmosphere, and Magnetosphere
items.summary: Michael RubensteinNorthwestern University, Chicago We propose a mission to use ~10,000 actively steerable femtosats to map the ring composition, atmospheric composition and density, and the magnetic field distribution of Saturn. items.summary: Marco QuadrelliNASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory Humanity has never touched the particles of a planetary ring, but if we were able to get close enough and sample them, it will transform understanding of ring structure and origins for Saturn (dense), Uranus and Neptune (tenuous), rings of other obj

items.title: ECLIPSE – Efficient variable Conductivity Lunar Insulator for Passive Surveyor Environmental control
items.summary: Austin Phoenix Virginia Polytechnic Institute & State A disaggregated lunar infrastructure requires novel thermal management methods to enable future lunar operations. While thermal management solutions exist for large infrastructure, the smaller mobile systems that operate independently re

items.title: Plasmon-Enhanced Radioisotope Thermophotovoltaic (PRTPV) Power Generation for Interstellar Missions
items.summary: Keunhan ParkUniversity of Utah This proposal seeks to develop a transformative energy system — the plasmon-enhanced radioisotope Thermophotovoltaic (PRTPV) generator — that significantly surpasses the performance of current radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) in both specific p

items.title: Trust, connection are key ingredients for Hawaiʻi's food security
items.summary: When talking about Hawaiʻi's food security, the conversation often focuses on physical infrastructure—warehouses, cold storage and doubling crop production to reduce the need to import 90% of food. A new policy article by a University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa researcher contends that physical infras

items.title: From quantum error correction to emergent gravity: Probing holographic universes at QLab
items.summary: One of the deepest ambitions in modern physics is understanding how the fabric of space and time could emerge from fundamental quantum degrees of freedom to establish a quantum theory of gravity.

items.title: Cosmic gymnastics: How the Milky Way once underwent a dramatic flip
items.summary: The Milky Way may have undergone a dramatic change in orientation during its history, according to new research that helps explain a long-standing mystery about our galaxy. Using supercomputer simulations of galaxies like the Milky Way, a team of astronomers from Durham University found that galaxie

items.title: New geopolitical-economic order framework shows firm strategies vary under two types of state-market order
items.summary: A new paper published in Global Strategy Journal develops a geopolitical-economic order framework suggesting that the extent of geopolitical competition incentivizes states to create one of two types of economic order: a market-based order built on laissez-faire and mutually beneficial voluntary exc

items.title: New NASA Earth missions gear up to start science flights
items.summary: Landslides in Alaska. Air quality in Atlanta. Fire clouds out West. From the Arctic fringes to farm country, NASA's newest class of suborbital Earth Venture missions is gearing up to deliver science that will benefit communities in the United States and beyond.

items.title: New 'demon' cavefish lineage discovered in Alabama cave
items.summary: For decades, scientists have crawled through Bobcat Cave on Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, searching its dark pools and underground passages for rare life. The cave is home to the endangered Alabama cave shrimp, making it one of the most closely watched cave systems in the region. Researchers have

items.summary: *Notice* The Payment Management System (PMS) is currently experiencing issues affecting Federal Financial Report (FFR) generation. Impacted NASA grant recipients will not be considered noncompliant. NASA is working with PMS to resolve the issue. Updates will be posted here. Grants Status Requests To

items.title: How community-led health and livelihood programs helped protect rainforests during the pandemic
items.summary: As COVID-19 disrupted rural economies, communities living near protected rainforests faced rising uncertainty about jobs, health and basic needs. A new study in The Lancet Planetary Health shows that when communities were supported by health and livelihood programs they designed with local nongovern

items.title: Geophysics brings clarity to the potential of natural hydrogen
items.summary: Natural hydrogen is considered a potential low-carbon energy source. But how much is generated underground—and how quickly? An international research team led by the LIAG Institute for Applied Geophysics in Hannover has published the first process-based estimates for the Western Pyrenees and Norther

items.title: Establishing Crew Exposure Limits of Martian Dust
items.summary: Background Human exploration of Mars will expose crews to a persistent, fine particulate environment whose physicochemical properties and health implications remain only partly understood. Because no samples of authentic Martian airborne dust have been returned to Earth, NASA must rely on lunar dust

items.title: Listening in on toads reveals expansion secrets
items.summary: Analysis of more than 3 million toad calls from locations across Australia gives rare insights into toad breeding behavior—and offers new hope for halting their advance. James Cook University ecology researchers discovered that the best environmental conditions for toad calling included low moonligh

items.title: New wasp species named after Ernest Hemingway amid efforts to control destructive sugar beet pest
items.summary: Earth remains full of undiscovered species and unexplored interactions that continue to fascinate us, including, in the most ordinary places, the fields where our food is grown.

items.title: A star orbiting our galaxy's supermassive black hole tests the limits of relativity
items.summary: Although general relativity (GR) is an elegant theory of gravity, we don't often need to use it. For most gravitational interactions, Newton's theory of universal gravitation works just fine. Newton's model is good enough to navigate spacecraft through the solar system and for most st

items.title: Watching too much TV in midlife may shrink the brain
items.summary: Watching television very often in midlife may be linked to troubling brain changes decades later. In a study of about 1,700 adults, frequent TV viewers later showed smaller brain regions involved in memory, decision-making, and visual processing, along with more signs of damage in the brain’s white

items.title: A fairer world without destroying the planet? It's possible
items.summary: Imagine a future where everyone has enough, on a planet that stays livable. A new report makes the case that it's within reach.

items.title: Triassic inferno: Fires raged across Europe for millennia during past global warming
items.summary: The end-Triassic mass extinction, 201 million years ago, is linked to volcanism that resulted from the break-up of Pangea and which pumped out vast amounts of CO2 into the atmosphere, causing extreme global warming of 5 to 10 degrees Celsius. As a result, tree-dominated vegetation collapsed and the

items.title: New film highlights ESRC research on online harms
items.summary: HateLab, led by Professor Matthew Williams of Cardiff University, has shown that spikes in online hate speech can be followed by increases in hate crime offline.

items.title: Mars's 2018 planetwide dust storm hints at possible solar influence on its weather
items.summary: Solar storms may affect weather on Mars when they strike at the same time as major Martian dust storms, a new study suggests for the first time. Researchers found evidence of rising temperatures in the red planet's lower atmosphere when the two events coincided—hinting that Mars may have a more

items.title: A New Compact Instrument Enables High-Fidelity Measurements of Energetic Particles on CubeSats
items.summary: A team of NASA-sponsored scientists and engineers has developed a novel approach to observing high-energy particles in the near-Earth space environment, incorporating miniaturized sensors into a compact, multi-view particle detection instrument unlike any before it. Built for NASA’s Relativistic Ele

items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari as world’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
items.summary: Ancient sediment reveals that some of K’gari’s deepest lakes vanished around 7,500 years ago, despite unusually heavy rainfall. The surprising discovery suggests changing winds and future climate shifts could threaten the sacred lakes known as the Eyes of K’gari.

items.title: Upside of online relationships
items.summary: Last fall, students filed into Vilas Hall for the first session of Online Communication and Personal Relationships, a class with the potential to improve their friendships and family relationships. There was even a chance it could help them meet the love of their lives while boosting their mental he

items.title: Students' emotional resources in high school linked to college completion
items.summary: Academic achievement in high school has long been viewed as one of the strongest predictors of college success. But a new study from Penn State researchers suggests that students' emotional resources—including their motivation, persistence and perceptions of school climate—may also play an impo

items.title: AI agent helps prepare synchrotron X-ray experimental measurements, paving the way for autonomous operation
items.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) models are now used daily by many people worldwide, both for professional and personal purposes. Over the past decades, scientists specialized in various disciplines have also started using these models to conduct research or simplify their experimental practices.

items.title: Invisible microbes 'mining' toxic waste in the Flinders Ranges
items.summary: New research from Monash University revealed that microscopic life is actively breaking down decades-old mining waste in South Australia, turning stable radioactive and toxic metals into mobile nanoparticles that can easily travel through the environment.

items.title: The best way to measure fire risk might be underground
items.summary: When fire risk is high, Arizonans know the major warning signs: hot temperatures, low humidity and lots of wind.

items.title: For consumers, distrust outweighs trust
items.summary: Anyone who has opened the door of a vacation rental after a long trip, only to find it hasn't been cleaned as promised, knows the burn of betrayal. That traveler is more likely to distrust the proprietor of their next vacation rental, according to new research from Annabelle Roberts, assistant

items.title: Astronomers find the first atmosphere on a rocky world in the habitable zone
items.summary: Astronomers have found the first confirmed atmosphere around a rocky planet in another star’s habitable zone. The planet, LHS 1140 b, revealed its atmosphere through helium slowly leaking into space. Located 48 light-years away, the world may have preserved its atmosphere for billions of years, maki

items.title: Aligned graphite particles unlock stable levitation above magnets, study finds
items.summary: A diamagnetic substance is slightly repelled by magnetic fields. With a strong enough magnet, the diamagnetic force can override gravity, and the substance will float in the air. Graphite, the main component of pencil lead, is considered one of the best substances for such real-world levitation, and

items.title: Astronomers catch a recurring black hole eruption in real time
items.summary: For the third time in 35 years, the black hole at the center of the galaxy IC 3599 has erupted, and this time, astronomers were watching it closely. IC 3599, a galaxy roughly 280 million light-years away, has flared this dramatically twice before, in 1990 and 2010, but both eruptions were only piece

items.title: Indigenous knowledge key to building healthier and more sustainable food systems
items.summary: Traditional and Indigenous knowledge could play a vital role in addressing some of the world's most pressing challenges, including climate change, biodiversity loss and poor health and nutrition, according to a new review of current research.

items.title: Ancient DNA reveals a chilling secret at China’s 4,000-year-old Shimao city
items.summary: Ancient DNA from China’s vast 4,000-year-old Shimao settlement is revealing how one of East Asia’s earliest complex societies was organized. Researchers found that its people were largely descended from local Yellow River farming communities, but also had genetic ties to distant southern and steppe

items.title: Great Barrier Reef at risk of 'collapse', scientists warn
items.summary: Australia's Great Barrier Reef is at very high risk of extreme and widespread bleaching and "ecosystem collapse" linked to this year's El Niño weather phenomenon, leading scientists warned Tuesday.

items.title: A hidden “switch-off” signal could predict solar storms seven years early
items.summary: A newly discovered turning point in the Sun’s 11-year cycle could allow scientists to predict future solar activity years earlier than before. At this “switch-off” point, the most violent space weather abruptly ends, and the remaining number of sunspots offers clues about the next cycle’s strength.

items.title: Znanstvenici razvili metodu uzgoja umjetnih krvnih žila
items.summary: ISTRAŽIVAČI s MIT-a razvili su metodu za precizan uzgoj umjetnih krvnih žila pomoću magnetskih sila. Ova tehnika omogućuje kontrolu smjera i duljine rasta kapilara, što je ključno za tkivni inženjering.

items.title: Fires in Brazil's Amazon dropped to historic low in 2025
items.summary: Forest fires in the Brazilian Amazon fell to a historic low in 2025 after peaking the previous year, according to a report published Tuesday.

items.title: Scientists found mystery bacteria inside one of the ocean’s most elusive whales
items.summary: Scientists studying decades of pygmy sperm whale strandings have uncovered three previously unknown types of Helicobacter bacteria hiding in the animals’ stomachs. All four infected whales showed serious digestive damage, including inflammation, ulcers, scarring, and parasite infestations, although

items.title: New molecular map reveals how the flu virus hijacks human cells
items.summary: Scientists uncovered how influenza A commandeers human cells, including a surprising strategy that dissolves tiny structures in the nucleus and releases proteins the virus may use to reproduce. The detailed molecular map could reveal new drug targets and help researchers study dangerous flu strains

items.title: Why Maine’s Sandy Shorelines Turn Jagged
items.summary: Differences in the underlying bedrock and how rivers distribute sediment make the coastlines west of Portland look unlike those northeast of the city.

items.title: This food compound may help vaccines work better in older adults
items.summary: A naturally occurring compound found in foods such as wheat germ, mushrooms, and aged cheese may help older adults build stronger protection after vaccination. In a small pilot trial, researchers gave spermidine supplements to adults over 65 following their third COVID-19 vaccine dose. The biggest i

items.title: Dying Sun-like stars may kick themselves through space
items.summary: Dying Sun-like stars may not fade away quietly. As blobs of gas erupt unevenly from their swollen surfaces, each burst gives the star a tiny push in the opposite direction. Thousands of these random kicks could eventually break apart distant stellar pairs or, in rare cases, drive two stars into a vi

items.title: Memorable visuals drive online ad purchases more than emotion, study suggests
items.summary: A study of digital advertising in the International Journal of Business and Systems Research suggests that memorable visuals are more likely to encourage consumers to buy than campaigns that rely primarily on emotional impact. The findings challenge conventional wisdom about how online advertising i

items.title: New quantum gravity theory links entropy, dark energy, and life
items.summary: A new theoretical study offers a possible explanation for how the Universe can grow more complex without violating the second law of thermodynamics. Using a quantum gravity framework called Gravity from Entropy, mathematician Ginestra Bianconi found that the Universe’s total entropy may rise as spac

items.title: Envisioning the next 150 years of crop research with the Alma Mater Plots
items.summary: A couple of miles south of the main quad at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, a remarkable agricultural experiment is taking shape. The so-called Alma Mater Plots are unique in their setup—with 64 plots of 1 acre (0.4 hectares) each, individually tile-drained, it is the largest experiment

items.title: Q&A: How anaerobic digestion can help reduce emissions and increase income streams
items.summary: An advanced technology that could benefit farmers' bottom line and help meet the rising demand for domestic energy production borrows a key ingredient from a centuries-old practice of burning dried manure to warm homes where trees were sparse or expensive: animal waste.

items.title: Eight lifesaving ways to improve wildfire risk management
items.summary: As the flames advanced toward the village of Bédar in Spain's Almería province, the mayor gave a desperate order to ring the church bells to warn residents. It was not enough. The July 9 Los Gallardos fire, which claimed 13 lives and burned 7,000 hectares (17,300 acres), was Spain's third-

items.title: Decade of research reshapes multilingual learner policy
items.summary: A lot of high school students load up on Advanced Placement courses to strengthen their college applications. But for many California students classified as English learners, access to even the basic college-preparatory courses required for admission to the state's public universities can remai

items.title: Students enable widespread water monitoring in India
items.summary: When a local community gets involved in environmental monitoring, its efforts can build awareness of local environmental issues and provide more abundant data than scientists can collect on their own. But how does the quality of the data compare with readings taken by professionals?

items.title: Unusual three-way bestows magnetic sense to single-celled organism
items.summary: How do microorganisms orient themselves to find the optimal habitat? Scientists have thoroughly investigated so-called magnetotactic bacteria, which use the Earth's magnetic field as a biological compass. Some eukaryotic single-celled organisms—that is, more complex organisms like ciliates, whi

items.title: One idea, two cosmic mysteries—linking Little Red Dots and globular clusters
items.summary: A new study led by astronomers at The University of Texas at Austin proposes a theory that could solve two astronomical riddles at once: the nature of Little Red Dots and the origin of globular clusters. Rather than representing distinct objects, the study suggests that one may instead be the ancest

items.title: Transforming vibrations into clean fuels and chemicals through piezosynthesis
items.summary: From ocean waves and flowing rivers to the systems used to transport and treat water, vibrations are everywhere. But can vibrations do more than just shake water? Can they split water or drive the production of useful chemicals from water? A research team led by Professor Sai Kishore Ravi from the S

items.title: New cell imaging method measures protein folding in living organelles
items.summary: Protein aggregates are associated with the development of diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. These are found more often in certain areas of cells known as membraneless organelles. Do these organelles promote this protein clumping, or do they protect the cell by temporarily absorbin

items.title: 'Narcissist' bosses are more likely to oppose work from home, studies show. But are they right?
items.summary: Have you ever wondered why your boss wants you back in the office full-time, rather than allowing hybrid work, where you can combine work from home and in an office? A fascinating study just shed fresh light on this topic.

items.title: 30,000 people tried for bigamy over the past 400 years in England and Wales, archives reveal
items.summary: Bigamy has been a rare crime during the past 400 years—and offenders have been harshly punished. It seems likely that no more than 30,000 individuals have ever been tried for bigamy in England and Wales during this period. Most have been convicted, and possible punishment has ranged from execution,

items.title: Molecules stop carbon nanotubes clumping, unlocking record heat-to-electricity performance
items.summary: QUT researchers have overcome a challenge that has limited next-generation energy-harvesting materials for more than two decades, opening the door to more powerful wearable electronics and new ways of turning wasted heat into electricity. The breakthrough centers on carbon nanotubes, which are flexi

items.title: Carbon nanotube model reveals how smart sensors perform at aircraft-like extremes
items.summary: Researchers from Skoltech, in collaboration with the Harbin Institute of Technology and Jiangsu University (China), have published a study presenting the first empirical model to explain and predict the sensing behavior of hierarchical tri-phase carbon nanotube systems across a wide temperature rang

items.title: Early career funding improves scientific influence and citations for women
items.summary: Early-career funding has a bigger impact on women than men when it comes to their careers in science, according to a new study from City St George's, University of London. While the study found that successful applicants in both groups secure more future research funding than unsuccessful appli

items.title: Ancestral variation, not new mutations, may impact survival of species facing climate change
items.summary: Research co-authored by an Oklahoma Museum of Natural History curator, her graduate student and an international team of scientists has shed new light on the evolutionary "toolkit" animals use to survive environmental change.

items.title: Heat shock reveals rapid protein-control system cells may use to survive
items.summary: The planet is getting hotter, and life will need to adapt to rising temperatures to survive. One way cells adapt to changing conditions is by synthesizing new proteins. That works well when the environment changes slowly, but if a crisis hits, like a sudden temperature increase, cells cannot wait fo

items.title: How rainfall timing can dramatically reshape entire plant communities
items.summary: Every spring, millions of people around the world head outdoors to admire wildflowers. But flowering is much more than a beautiful seasonal display. It is one of the most important stages in a plant's life cycle, determining not only its ability to reproduce but also the survival of pollinators

items.title: Light-driven chemistry steers electron transfers beyond redox limits
items.summary: Chemists use single-electron transfers to synthesize complex, ring-shaped molecular structures found in many drug candidates and advanced materials, but current techniques still have limitations. A new study, accepted for publication in Nature, describes a technique that could steer these chemical r

items.title: Acid-resistant nanocage shows promise for targeted gastric cancer therapy
items.summary: Researchers from the Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore (NUS Medicine), have developed a swallowable nanoscale delivery platform designed to transport therapeutic enzymes through the stomach's acidic environment and activate a cancer-killing reaction at tumor site

items.title: Oystercatchers, known for sharp bills and beady eyes, are making a comeback thanks to conservation
items.summary: On a pristine Rhode Island beach teeming with noisy seabirds, a group of biologists armed with nets sneak up on a pair of American oystercatcher chicks.

items.title: Znanstvenici: Majmuni imaju geometrijske vještine slične djeci
items.summary: NOVO istraživanje pokazuje da majmuni imaju geometrijske vještine slične ljudskim predškolcima, što upućuje na zajedničke kognitivne temelje. Formalno obrazovanje nadograđuje te urođene sposobnosti.
